channel.title: Smart Money Digest — Daily Tape
channel.description: Fresh activist 13Ds and conviction insider buys, trading-day mornings.

item.title: 51Talk's CEO Just Dropped $10.5M Into His Own Stock
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Jack Huang writes a ten-million-dollar check on COE while Glenbrook opens an activist SenesTech stake and Energizer's biggest holder keeps buying.
item.content:encoded:
Jack Huang writes a ten-million-dollar check on COE while Glenbrook opens an activist SenesTech stake and Energizer's biggest holder keeps buying.

The only headline print on the tape today is a big one. Jack Jiajia Huang, CEO of COE (51Talk Online Education Group), bought $10.5 million of his own stock on July 14. Five tranches, 622,140 shares, prices from $16.56 to $17.23. That is a Form 4 (the filing an insider must submit within two days of trading their own company's shares).

Nobody accidentally buys ten million dollars. When the boss of a beaten-down Chinese education name writes a check that size, I want to know what he sees that the tape doesn't.

Now the fresh 13D. SNES (SenesTech): Glenbrook Capital Management filed a Schedule 13D (a stake over 5% carrying activist intent, versus the passive 13G) on the rodent-birth-control microcap. A real fund taking a disclosed activist position in a company this small usually wants something to change. Filing.

Skip the other two 13Ds. Michael Field on HST Global and an individual on Metals Royalty are microcap plumbing, not smart money.

ENR (Energizer Holdings): the Ramirez group (Aqua Capital and a cluster of affiliated foundations), already a 10% owner (they hold more than a tenth of the company), added $2.43 million across seven buys at $19.67 to $20.96 on July 21. Quiet accumulation by the biggest holder of a name sitting near multi-year lows is exactly what this letter hunts for.

ARTV (Artiva Biotherapeutics): RA Capital Management bought $2.58 million and amended its 13D the same day. RA is one of the sharpest biotech crossover shops around, and when it adds to a clinical-stage name near $9.50 while refreshing its activist filing, that is conviction, not a rebalance. Filing.

Then the clusters (multiple insiders buying inside the same window, the cleanest signal on the whole tape). RBKB (Rhinebeck Bancorp): four insiders, CEO Matthew Smith, directors Nancy Patzwahl and Suzanne Loughlin, and commercial-banking head Michael Vitale, bought $650k combined at roughly $12 on July 22. Four checks into a sleepy Hudson Valley thrift on the same day is loud. BYRN (Byrna Technologies): CEO Conn Davis plus directors TJ Kennedy and Herbert Hughes put in $253k around $3.45 on the less-lethal weapons maker.

Ignore the noise. A SPAC sponsor bought $6.9 million of its own $10.00 units and a UAW retiree trust dollar-cost-averaged $9.9 million into a non-traded BDC. Both are mechanics running on autopilot, not anyone making a bet.

Data window July 22 to 24, 2026. Not investment advice.

item.title: RA Capital piles $6.9M into Parabilis while a whole Jersey boardroom buys at $10
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Peter Kolchinsky's fund keeps adding to a small-cap biotech, sixteen Columbia Financial insiders subscribe in lockstep, and a $93M CEO "buy" turns out to mean less than the number suggests.
item.content:encoded:
Peter Kolchinsky's fund keeps adding to a small-cap biotech, sixteen Columbia Financial insiders subscribe in lockstep, and a $93M CEO "buy" turns out to mean less than the number suggests.

No whale 13Ds, no early 13Fs, no Burry. Straight from SEC EDGAR, the real signal today lives in the insider tape.

Start with RA Capital. Peter Kolchinsky and Rajeev Shah's healthcare fund (a marquee biotech crossover shop, the kind that follows a drug program from private rounds into the public float) bought $6.9M of PBLS (Parabilis Medicines) on July 20. Six tranches, 244,593 shares, prices climbing from $27.13 to $30.00. They already sit on the board as a 10% owner, and they paid up into strength rather than waiting for a dip. When the people with the deepest read on a small-cap pipeline add this aggressively, I listen.

Now the cluster. CLBK (Columbia Financial) logged sixteen insiders buying $4.77M, every single share at exactly $10.00. That price flatness means a subscription in the New Jersey thrift's stock offering, not open-market conviction. Even so, this is the whole house writing personal checks. CEO Thomas Kemly put in $530k, CFO Thomas Splaine $500k, COO Steven Klein $500k, and the full board lined up behind them. A conversion buy is softer than chasing the tape, but sixteen executives funding their own recapitalization still tells you where they think book value goes from here.

The biggest number today is a mirage. Sterling Real Estate Trust CEO Kenneth Regan "bought" $92.9M (3,644,624 units at $25.50) on July 21. Sterling is a non-traded REIT, so that $25.50 is a fixed NAV subscription price, not a market bid anyone else can hit. Impressive line, zero tradable signal. Move on.

The smaller prints worth clocking: Hartree Partners added $392k to HDSN (Hudson Technologies) as a 10% owner, staying long the refrigerant-pricing story. Navios chief Angeliki Frangou bought another $253k of NMM (Navios Maritime Partners), a name she scoops up on nearly every pullback. Jorey Chernett took $95k more of TOI (Oncology Institute) as a 10% owner.

On the activist side, Saba Capital amended its 13D (the filing a 5%-plus owner uses to flag intent) on BlackRock ESG Capital Allocation Trust, and Legion Partners amended on NN Inc. Both are known agitators. Read these as housekeeping unless a later filing shows they raised their stakes, which today's does not.

The trade of the day is one name. RA Capital wants more Parabilis, and they know it better than you do.

Data from SEC EDGAR filings, July 21 to July 23, 2026. Not investment advice.

item.title: Energizer's Biggest Outside Holder Just Swept $2M of Stock at $20
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The Ramirez vehicles bought 100,000 ENR shares in five clean blocks, and Conagra's CEO put half a million of his own money into a 14-handle the same day.
item.content:encoded:
The Ramirez vehicles bought 100,000 ENR shares in five clean blocks, and Conagra's CEO put half a million of his own money into a 14-handle the same day.

No whale alerts today. The 13D column is garbage. But the Form 4 tape (the report an officer, director, or 5%-plus owner files after trading their own stock) handed over two prints I would not skip.

The print of the day

The Ramirez family vehicles, filing as one 10% owner (Aqua Capital, Durango Capital, and a stack of Panamanian foundations), bought 100,000 shares of ENR on July 17. Five clean 20,000-share blocks between $19.94 and $20.51. Call it $2.02 million.

That is four times the size of anything else on this tape. Energizer's biggest disclosed outside holder is not trimming into weakness, he is stepping in front of it at a 20-handle. On a levered consumer staple, that is a balance-sheet vote, and it is the loudest one here.

The CEO check

John Brase, President and CEO of Conagra, bought 35,000 shares of CAG at $14.5895 on July 17. $510,632 of his own cash.

Open-market buying from a sitting CEO is the cleanest version of this signal, because nobody handed it to him. Conagra at fourteen dollars is a name the market has written off. He wants more of it.

Skip the 13D column

One new SCHEDULE 13D crossed. Research Alliance Holdings IV filed on Research Alliance Corp IV. That is a SPAC sponsor disclosing it owns its own SPAC. Plumbing. https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/2137777/0002137786-26-000012-index.html

The amendments were founders papering their own stock (Berkley on Berkley, Rothberg on Butterfly Network) with one that isn't. Tempus AI amended its 13D on Personalis on July 21. A strategic acquirer updating a declared stake in a genomics company is a different animal than a founder filing on himself. https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1527753/0001193125-26-310289-index.html

The rest of the tape

CV CapsoVision: 10% owner Eliyahou Harari and affiliates took 61,068 shares at $6.73 on July 17, $410,988. Second-biggest buy of the day in a name almost nobody covers.

PRQR ProQR: director Bart Filius bought 150,000 shares at $1.7195, $257,925. Sub-$2 biotech, quarter-million-dollar director check. Real money at that price.

TSM: VP Shyue-Shyh Lin bought 3,000 ADRs across three fills on July 19 at $71.32 to $72.10, $215,360.

GF and MXF: Saba Capital kept grinding into closed-end funds on July 20, $258,790 of New Germany Fund and $163,990 of Mexico Fund. Saba does one thing, which is buy the discount and then force it shut. Routine. It still compounds.

CHCO City Holding: four directors, Fisher, Strong-Treister, Hoyer, and Reyes, all bought at $134.55 on July 21 for $59,740 combined. That is a cluster buy (two or more insiders hitting the tape inside a few days), and here the headcount says more than the dollars. Sixty grand across four board members is a nod, not a bet.

Filings from July 20 to July 22, 2026, straight from SEC EDGAR. Not investment advice.

item.title: 51Talk's CEO Just Bought $8.5M of His Own Stock
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Jack Huang backed up the truck on COE across three fills, and a software company filed a 13D on SPAR Group.
item.content:encoded:
Jack Huang backed up the truck on COE across three fills, and a software company filed a 13D on SPAR Group.

No whale alerts on this tape. Two days of filings, one strategic 13D worth your time, and one CEO writing a very large personal check.

A software company just filed a 13D on SPAR Group

ReposiTrak filed a fresh SCHEDULE 13D on SPAR Group on July 20. A 13D is the form you file when you cross 5% of a company and intend to actually do something about it, as opposed to the passive 13G that index funds file by the truckload.

What makes this one interesting is that the filer is not a fund at all. It is an operating company, a supply chain compliance software outfit, taking a declared stake in a retail merchandising services business it plausibly wants to own. Strategics do not cross 5% for the dividend.

Filing: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/50471/0001437749-26-023888-index.html

The other two new 13Ds are founders filing on their own paper, Konstantakopoulos at Costamare Bulkers and Thien Chiet Chai at Founder Group. Internal plumbing. Ignore both.

The insider tape

Jack Jiajia Huang, CEO of 51Talk Online Education, bought 495,360 shares of COE across three fills on July 9 at $16.71 to $17.24. Call it $8.48 million of his own money. That is the print of the day and it is not close. A Form 4 (the disclosure an officer or director files after trading their own stock) showing a CEO buying eight figures on the open market is the highest-quality signal this tape produces, and Huang paid up into the offer rather than lowballing bids.

Fairmount Funds Management took 853,450 shares of CBIO at $14.50 on July 16, about $12.4 million, plus a second tranche of 525,897 shares struck at a tenth of a cent. Fairmount sits as both director and 10% owner at Crescent Biopharma, which puts them on the same reporting hook as management. Their 13D/A the same day is just the paperwork catching up. Dedicated healthcare money adding size at $14.50 is a real vote.

Michael Heinrich, executive chairman of ZeroStack, picked up 4,608,575 shares of ZSTK at $0.7549 on July 20, roughly $3.48 million. Seven-figure check from the chairman, sub-dollar tape. The size is real. So is what the price is telling you.

RA Capital added 160,354 shares of ARTV around $8.93 on July 16, about $1.43 million. Kevin Tang and Tang Capital bought 167,616 shares of FULC at $3.52 to $3.56, roughly $595,000. Tang buys busted biotech for the cash sitting on the balance sheet, so when he accumulates, somebody has already run the liquidation math.

Outside biotech there was one print worth naming. Director Homer Livingston III bought 26,343 shares of FCEL at $18.79, about $495,000.

Now the one to throw out. Lincoln Financial put $25 million into LNBIX, its own Bain Capital credit fund. Biggest dollar figure on the tape and the least informative thing on it. That is seed capital, not conviction.

No cluster buys today, meaning no company had two or more insiders buying inside the window. One CEO going big is what you get.

Filings from July 19 to July 21, 2026, straight from SEC EDGAR. Not investment advice.

item.title: Sumitomo Drops Another $319M on Jefferies
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Japan's largest bank keeps buying its Wall Street partner, Prudential loads $60M of energy infrastructure, and Elevance's CEO steps in as managed care bleeds.
item.content:encoded:
Japan's largest bank keeps buying its Wall Street partner, Prudential loads $60M of energy infrastructure, and Elevance's CEO steps in as managed care bleeds.

One print towers over everything else on the tape.

Sumitomo Mitsui Financial Group bought 5,906,542 shares of JEF at $53.96 on July 15, a $318.7M open-market buy disclosed on a Form 4 (the filing any board member or 10%-plus owner must submit within two days of trading). Japan's biggest bank sits on Jefferies' board and has spent years building this alliance from a handshake into a real ownership stake. A third of a billion dollars in a single day is not a rebalance. It is a strategic partner leaning harder into the relationship at a price it clearly likes.

That is the read of the day. Everything below is smaller.

The one 13D worth your time

Poplar Point Capital Management filed a fresh SCHEDULE 13D on TransAct Technologies (TACT), the filing an investor must submit within 10 days of crossing 5% with intent to influence the company. Poplar Point is a concentrated value shop, and TransAct is a beaten-down maker of casino and food-service tech. A patient fund putting its name on the line at a micro-cap this quiet is the kind of setup that pays off in quarters, not days. Filing: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1588273/0001398344-26-012268-index.html

Ignore the Columbus Circle SPAC sponsor filing. That is plumbing.

The insider tape

Prudential Financial bought $60M of Tortoise Energy Infrastructure (TYG) at a flat $10, two 3-million-share tranches on July 15. As a 10% owner accumulating a closed-end energy pipeline fund, Prudential is quietly telling you where it wants yield and hard-asset exposure right now.

Managed care has been a graveyard, which makes the Elevance Health (ELV) cluster interesting. CEO Gail Boudreaux put up $1.0M across two buys at $366 to $368, and director Ramiro Peru added $366k. When the sitting CEO buys seven figures of her own hammered stock in the open market, that is conviction you can measure.

Decisive Point Group bought $19.1M of Standard Nuclear (STDN) at $15. Another 10%-owner bet on the nuclear buildout that keeps drawing serious money.

Two quick hits. PIMCO's Christian Stracke bought $1.64M of the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI), a credit heavyweight backing his own book. And Liberty Latin America chairman Michael Fries added $1.0M of LILA at $20.39.

What to skip: Manulife shuffled $79M between four of its own affiliates into the John Hancock GA Mortgage Trust. That is insurance plumbing, not a signal. Same for German American Bancorp's directors matching $20k apiece.

Data window July 16 to July 20, 2026, straight from SEC EDGAR. Not investment advice.

item.title: Two Dollar-Stock Directors Just Wrote Million-Dollar Checks
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Cardiff Oncology and Vivani insiders each put $1M into sub-$1.50 biotech, while Thrivent quietly dropped $4M into an Ares credit fund.
item.content:encoded:
Cardiff Oncology and Vivani insiders each put $1M into sub-$1.50 biotech, while Thrivent quietly dropped $4M into an Ares credit fund.

No whale 13Ds, no early 13Fs, no Scion. So the story is the insider tape, and it is louder than the price tags suggest.

CRDF (Cardiff Oncology) is the real print. Director Gary Pace bought 687,285 shares for a clean $1,000,000 at $1.455 on July 14, and CEO Mani Mohindru added $50,000 the same day. Two insiders, one date, roughly $1.05M into a microcap oncology name trading under $1.50. A cluster (multiple insiders buying the same window) at a director's own million-dollar size is about as direct a signal as this tape produces. When the board writes personal checks this big into a broken chart, they either know something or they are very stubborn.

VANI (Vivani Medical) is the echo. Director and 10% owner Gregg Williams took down 793,650 shares for $999,999 at $1.26 on July 15. Same playbook, different name. A near-million from someone who already owns a tenth of the company is not a token gesture.

Now the big dollar figure, with an asterisk. ARDC (Ares Dynamic Credit Allocation Fund) saw Thrivent Financial for Lutherans, a 10% owner, buy 160,000 shares for $4,000,000 flat at $25 on July 14. Real money, but it is an institution accumulating a closed-end credit fund at NAV, not a founder betting on his own turnaround. Read it as steady credit demand, not a conviction spike.

Same flavor over at Diameter Dynamic Credit Fund, where Co-President Scott Goodwin put $1,250,000 into his own vehicle at $10.06. Insider seeding the fund. Nice to see, not a stock call.

One name to file away: Tang Capital, Kevin Tang's biotech shop, kept nibbling BOLD (Boundless Bio) as a 10% owner, roughly $155,000 across three prints at $2.43 to $2.44 on July 14. Small on its own, but Tang tends to keep buying.

On the 13D side it was closed-end-fund activism and community banks, not household names. Karpus Management filed on an AllianceBernstein muni income fund, and bank specialist Kenneth Lehman filed on BAFN (BayFirst Financial) here. Biotech fund HBM Healthcare filed on CMMB (Chemomab) here. Worth a bookmark, not a chase.

Skip the Sterling Real Estate Trust unit buys (flat $25.50 plan purchases) and the sub-$4k plumbing.

Data window July 15 to July 17, 2026, from SEC EDGAR. Not investment advice.

item.title: Energizer's Biggest Outside Holder Just Bought $2M in the Open
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: No whale 13Ds worth chasing, but a handful of size buyers kept paying up: Energizer's top holder, Tang Capital in Fulcrum, and Rick Rieder buying his own fund.
item.content:encoded:
No whale 13Ds worth chasing, but a handful of size buyers kept paying up: Energizer's top holder, Tang Capital in Fulcrum, and Rick Rieder buying his own fund.

No whale 13Ds worth chasing today and no insider clusters. What is left is a short list of single prints where somebody with real size kept buying in the open. Those are the ones I actually read.

Start with ENR (Energizer Holdings). The 10% owner group tied to Alfredo Diez Ramirez (Aqua Capital and a stack of related vehicles, the single largest outside holder) bought 100,000 shares across five prints on July 13, all around $20.18, for just over $2 million. This is a Form 4, the filing an insider or 5%-plus owner files after trading, and it is a real open-market add at market price. When your biggest outside shareholder keeps paying up like this, that is conviction you can price.

Next, a fresh SCHEDULE 13D (a stake over 5% filed when the holder plans to get active) landed on VVOS (Vivos Therapeutics) from V-Co Investors 4 LLC on July 15. Pair it with the tape. 10% owner Michael Skaff disclosed buying 5.5 million shares of the same name at $0.456, roughly $3.2 million total. Two separate parties accumulating a sub-dollar sleep-apnea name in the same week is the kind of confluence I stop on, even if the price tag screams lottery ticket. Filing: SEC.

FULC (Fulcrum Therapeutics) is the one I like best. Tang Capital Management, Kevin Tang's shop and a serial biotech accumulator that tends to show up ahead of hard catalysts, added roughly $891k across four July 13 prints near $3.56. Tang does not dabble in a name he is not building.

Two more. Rick Rieder, BlackRock's fixed-income CIO, personally put $567k into ECAT (the firm's own ESG closed-end fund) at $15.86 on July 13. And former Corning CFO Tony Tripeny wrote a $1 million check for IPX (IperionX), the US titanium-metal play, as a director on July 14. A manager buying his own fund and a director going seven figures both rhyme with "this looks cheap to me."

What I am ignoring. The $10 million "purchase" by RoboStrategy president Andrew Kang in BOT is a suspiciously round number in a barely-public shell that reads like founder plumbing, not a signal. And Warren Buffett's Berkshire 13D/A is his usual annual share reshuffle for charity, no new money.

Data window July 14 to July 16, 2026. Not investment advice.

item.title: ICONIQ Buys $3.8M More Netskope in the Open Market Under $12.50
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: A pre-IPO backer of the cybersecurity name added to its own position at the open, the one print on today's tape that actually says something.
item.content:encoded:
A pre-IPO backer of the cybersecurity name added to its own position at the open, the one print on today's tape that actually says something.

Skip the 13D pile first. A 13D is the filing you make when you cross 5% with intent to do something. Today's two fresh ones are both plumbing. Brown Brothers Harriman and AMG New York Holdings both filed on the same AMG BBH Asset-Backed Credit Fund, which is affiliate housekeeping between related shops, not an activist landing. Garden Investment Management filed a real one on Midera Food Processing, an obscure micro-cap I have no track record to trust (filing). Note it, do not chase it.

Now the tape that matters.

On July 10, ICONIQ Strategic Partners bought 306,399 shares of NTSK (Netskope) across two lots at $12.26 and $12.42, about $3.77M. Read the raw feed and it looks like a two-insider cluster worth $7.5M. It is not. The exact same shares show up once under the ICONIQ fund entities and once under partner William Griffith, both filed as 10% owners on a Form 4 (the report an insider or big holder files after trading). One buy, double-counted. Call it $3.8M.

Here is why I care anyway. ICONIQ backed Netskope before it went public last year, and here they are adding in the open market at roughly $12, not taking a courtesy allocation. A crossover sponsor paying up for more of a name it already knows cold is the closest thing to conviction this tape offers.

Next, shipping. Georgios Feidakis, chairman of GLBS (Globus Maritime), picked up 221,166 shares for about $603k at $2.70 to $2.75 (dated June 18, filed now). A controlling shareholder putting $600k into his own $2 dry-bulk micro-cap clears our single-buy bar cleanly. Late, but real.

Then NMM (Navios Maritime Partners), where CEO and chairwoman Angeliki Frangou added 3,334 shares for about $252k around $75 on July 10. Frangou buys her own stock like a reflex, so discount it a notch, but a C-suite open-market add (that is the top brass buying, not options vesting) still counts.

One more for the biotech watchers. RA Capital, Peter Kolchinsky's shop, added about $194k of ARTV (Artiva Biotherapeutics) near $8.95 as a 10% owner. Small, and below our headline bar, but RA rarely nibbles without a thesis behind it.

Everything else is noise. Horizon Kinetics kept buying its one-share and few-hundred-dollar tokens of Texas Pacific Land and RENN Fund, Stilwell tossed $4,300 at Peoples Financial, and a handful of sub-$30k director buys dotted the small caps. Ignore all of it.

Data window July 13 to July 15, 2026, straight from SEC EDGAR. Not investment advice.

item.title: Four IperionX Insiders Buy Titanium the Same Day While 51Talk's CEO Drops $9.3M
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: A full-house cluster in a US titanium play leads the tape, a Chinese ADR founder writes the biggest check, and Commercial Metals' CEO steps in on steel.
item.content:encoded:
A full-house cluster in a US titanium play leads the tape, a Chinese ADR founder writes the biggest check, and Commercial Metals' CEO steps in on steel.

The tape's best print is not the biggest one. It's the cluster.

Four IPX (IperionX) insiders bought on July 10, and they did it together. Executive Chairman Todd Hannigan put in $1.88M, director Lorraine Martin added $1M, President Toby Symonds $500k, and CEO Anastasios Arima another $497k. That is $3.9M of open-market buying across the top of the house, all around $3.58 a share. When the whole C-suite and a director step in on the same day (a cluster buy, and the strongest insider signal there is), they are telling you the stock is cheap right here. IperionX is a US titanium producer aimed straight at defense and aerospace supply chains. This is the kind of critical-minerals name that shows up on insider tapes before it shows up on CNBC.

Now the big number. COE (51Talk Online Education) CEO Jack Huang bought $9.3M of his own stock on July 6, roughly 563,000 shares between $16.45 and $16.72. That dwarfs everything else on the tape in raw dollars. I hold Chinese ADRs to a higher bar because the governance is murkier and a founder can buy to prop up a floor. But $9.3M in a single session is not a gesture, it is a statement.

Two more single buys I like. CMC (Commercial Metals) President and CEO Peter Matt bought $504k on July 10 at $61.30. A steel-cycle boss buying his own shares in the open market is a clean, boring, useful signal. And GGAL (Grupo Financiero Galicia) director Silvestre Vila Moret took down $2.16M of the Argentine bank on July 7 around $5.40, a real vote that the Milei trade keeps running.

The rest in one breath. Energizer's (ENR) controlling Ramirez group added $1.23M near $20.50. A four-person C-suite cluster at disinformation-detection micro-cap CYAB (Cyabra) bought a combined $285k, small dollars but the entire management team at once. And Dreamland's (TDIC) CEO Wai Yue Seto both bought $2.4M and filed a fresh 13D (a disclosure of a stake above 5%) on July 13. Founder buying his own company, so I log it and move on. Filing: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/2041338/0001493152-26-032907-index.html

One to ignore. That $990k CAI (Caris Life Sciences) director buy is dated May 15, a two-month-late Form 4 (the insider-transaction filing), not fresh conviction. And activist Impactive Capital amended its ABG (Asbury Automotive) 13D with nothing that reads as a real escalation. Filing: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1144980/0000921895-26-001801-index.html

Data from SEC filings dated July 12 to July 14, 2026. Not investment advice.

item.title: Iconiq and a Netskope Director Drop $14M Into Their Own Stock
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Two 10% owners bought Netskope hand over fist, Pat Gelsinger's crew loaded Gloo, and Mission Produce drew a $7.9M vote of confidence.
item.content:encoded:
Two 10% owners bought Netskope hand over fist, Pat Gelsinger's crew loaded Gloo, and Mission Produce drew a $7.9M vote of confidence.

No whale 13Ds, no early 13Fs, no Burry. So the real tape today is insider buying, and it is loud in two names.

NTSK (Netskope, the cybersecurity outfit that went public last year) is the print. Two 10% owners (holders of more than a tenth of the stock, the people closest to the cap table) bought on the same day. Director William Griffith took down 610,291 shares for $7.2M at $11.82. ICONIQ Strategic Partners, the Divesh Makan vehicle, matched him almost share for share, 610,091 shares for another $7.2M. That is $14.4M of open-market conviction into a post-IPO name from the two insiders who know it best. When the pre-IPO backers buy the public stock instead of selling into a lockup expiry, I pay attention.

GLOO (Gloo Holdings) is the other one, and it comes with a name. Three insiders bought $6M at $3.25. CEO Scott Beck led with $3.5M, director Derek Green added $2M, and Executive Chair Pat Gelsinger, yes, the former Intel CEO, put in $500k. A CEO and his chairman writing personal checks at a buck-and-change stock is the kind of cluster (multiple insiders buying at once, the strongest insider signal there is) I want to see.

One more single buy earns the ink. AVO (Mission Produce, the avocado grower) drew a $7.9M purchase from Globalharvest Holdings, a 10% owner, 592,957 shares at $13.28. A strategic holder adding to a beaten-down ag name. Real money, one clip.

On the 13D tape (a filing that flags a 5%-plus stake with intent to influence), Alafi Capital opened a new position in CALC (CalciMedica, a clinical-stage biotech). Alafi has backed biotech for decades, so a fresh 13D there reads as accumulation, not a trade (filing). Israel's L.I.A. Pure Capital filed a new 13D on EVGN (Evogene, a micro-cap ag-genomics name), smaller and speculative (filing).

Everything else was plumbing. Viking Global amended its BridgeBio stake with no escalation to report, and the Lionsgate share moves from Mark Rachesky were internal reshuffling that never touched the open market. Ignore both.

Data window: July 9-13, 2026, straight from SEC EDGAR. Not investment advice.

item.title: The Biggest Buy on the Tape Is Avocados: $15.8M Into Mission Produce
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Globalharvest piles into Mission Produce, Lynrock Lake goes active on Yext, Renesas discloses a stake in SiTime, and Energy Fuels' CEO writes a near-million-dollar check.
item.content:encoded:
Globalharvest piles into Mission Produce, Lynrock Lake goes active on Yext, Renesas discloses a stake in SiTime, and Energy Fuels' CEO writes a near-million-dollar check.

The print of the day is produce. Globalharvest Holdings Venture Ltd, already a 10% owner of Mission Produce (AVO), bought 1,179,087 more shares on July 7 at $13.40 to $13.42. That is a $15.8 million check in a single session. The same day it amended its Schedule 13D (the form you file when you own over 5% and reserve the right to lean on management), so this is a control-sized holder still adding, on the record. Filing

Fresh 13Ds

Yext (YEXT). Lynrock Lake LP filed a Schedule 13D on July 9. A 13D is the active form. Funds that just want to own the stock file the passive version, so Lynrock is telling you it reserves the right to get loud at the search-and-listings software company. Filing

SiTime (SITM). Renesas Electronics America, the US arm of the Japanese chip giant, filed a 13D on the precision-timing chipmaker. Strategic corporates do not file active stakes in adjacent chip companies for fun. The menu here is short: deeper partnership or an eventual takeout. Filing

Skip the rest. Securitize drew two 13Ds from its own people, including CEO Carlos Domingo, which is post-listing paperwork. Peak XV registering its seed stake in Horizon Quantum is the same story. Ignore all of it.

The insider tape

Energy Fuels (UUUU) is the cluster of the day (two or more insiders buying at once, which is when Form 4s, the insider trade reports, actually mean something). President and CEO Ross Bhappu bought 74,000 shares at $13.08 on July 7, a $967,920 personal check. Director Bruce Hansen added $50,780 the next day. A CEO putting nearly a million of his own money into a uranium producer has my full attention.

Grupo Financiero Galicia (GGAL). Director Silvestre Vila Moret bought roughly 403,000 shares of the Argentine bank on June 26 at $5.11 to $5.27, $2.09 million total. It just hit the tape this week, and a director buying eight figures of pesos worth of his own bank in dollars is conviction.

Energizer (ENR). The holding entities tied to Alfredo Jose Diez Ramirez, a 10% owner group, added 80,000 shares at $20.25 to $20.81, $1.64 million. A major holder quietly feeding on a beaten-down consumer staple.

Two quick ones. WRAP got a small cluster, director John Shulman at $110,000 and Executive Chairman and CEO Scot Cohen at $23,914, and it only makes the cut because they bought together. NMM CEO Angeliki Frangou added another $249,016 of Navios Maritime Partners at $73.74 to $76.14. She buys her own units constantly, and the consistency is the point.

The AMG and Brown Brothers Harriman purchases in their own credit fund are seed-capital plumbing. Pass.

Data: SEC EDGAR filings, July 8 to July 10, 2026. Not investment advice.

item.title: 51Talk's CEO Just Put $23 Million Into His Own Stock
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: Jack Huang's seven-figure buying spree leads a tape with an $8 million avocado add and a Maryland thrift where four insiders wrote checks at once.
item.content:encoded:
Jack Huang's seven-figure buying spree leads a tape with an $8 million avocado add and a Maryland thrift where four insiders wrote checks at once.

One print on today's tape dwarfs everything else, so let's start there.

The insider tape

COE. Jack Jiajia Huang, CEO of 51Talk Online Education Group, bought roughly 1.44 million shares of his own company on June 29 at prices from $15.20 to $16.78. Total damage: $23.5 million. That is not a signaling nibble or an options exercise dressed up as conviction. That is a chief executive backing up the truck across seven separate blocks in a single session, paying up as the price moved against him. When the person with the best information in the building spends that kind of money at market, I pay attention.

AVO. Globalharvest Holdings Venture Ltd, already a 10% owner (holders that size must report their trades, which is why we see them), added 650,415 shares of Mission Produce at $12.73 on July 6. That is an $8.3 million add to an existing position in the avocado supplier, and big holders rarely reload into names they plan to trim.

EWSB. Four insiders at EWSB Bancorp bought a combined $1.7 million on June 29, led by President and CEO Charles Schmalz at $676,410. Every share went off at a flat $10.00, which tells me this is a thrift conversion offering rather than open-market buying. Still, management subscribing seven figures of their own money into their own bank is skin in the game, just discount the signal accordingly.

PRQR. ProQR Therapeutics CEO Daniel De Boer bought 150,000 shares at $1.59 on July 7, a $238,500 check in a beaten-down gene therapy name. Modest dollars, but CEOs buying sub-$2 biotech stock with their own cash is rarer than you would think.

Two prints you can ignore. Thrivent Financial dropped $10.5 million into CADEX, an interval credit fund, which is asset allocation, not conviction. And the Viking Acquisition Corp. II sponsor bought $3 million of VII units at $10.00, which is standard SPAC plumbing.

Fresh 13Ds

Two individuals, Seth Demsey and John Crystal, each filed a new 13D (a form filed when someone crosses 5% with intent to influence) on Corvex, Inc. on July 8, the same day (Demsey filing, Crystal filing). Twin same-day stakes from individuals usually means deal consideration just landed, not an activist campaign. File it under context.

Jason Sawyer also went active on GridAI Technologies Corp. (filing). An individual taking an active stake in a small grid-infrastructure name during an AI power buildout is the kind of filing that ages interestingly.

Filings from SEC EDGAR, July 7 to July 9, 2026. Not investment advice.

item.title: Allseas Takes Another 7.3 Million Shares of TMC
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: The deep-sea miner's biggest strategic backer keeps stacking stock, a Marchex co-founder goes active, and two First Trust officers put $8.4 million into their own funds.
item.content:encoded:
The deep-sea miner's biggest strategic backer keeps stacking stock, a Marchex co-founder goes active, and two First Trust officers put $8.4 million into their own funds.

The print that matters today is TMC. Allseas Group, the Swiss offshore contractor that is both The Metals Company's engineering partner and its largest strategic holder, reported acquiring another 7,305,567 shares on July 1 and amended its SCHEDULE 13D (the form a holder files when it owns over 5% with intent to influence the company) the same day. The Form 4 lists no price, which usually means a contractual or warrant transaction rather than open-market buying. Either way, the partner that actually builds the mining ships keeps converting its relationship into equity, and that is the kind of alignment I want to see in a story stock. Filing here.

One more fresh 13D deserves a line. Russell C. Horowitz, who co-founded MCHX (Marchex) and ran it for years, filed a brand-new 13D on July 7 rather than another passive amendment. Founder filings are usually paperwork, but a founder switching to an active schedule on a forgotten micro-cap is how take-private stories start. Filing here.

The rest of the 13D stack is skippable. John Calamos filed on his own firm's new hedged-strategies fund and the Osprey III sponsor filed on its own SPAC. That is plumbing, not positioning.

The insider tape

The biggest dollars are a cluster (multiple insiders buying the same name, historically the strongest insider signal) at First Trust. President Michael D. Peck and Treasurer Chad Eisenberg put a combined $6.5 million into VFLEX at $27.66 and another $1.9 million into FTECX at $9.42, buys dated back in April and only now hitting EDGAR. I discount fund officers seeding their own vehicles. It is real skin in the game, but it is a business commitment, not a market call.

The cleaner signals are smaller:

  • PSBD: Martin C. Bicknell, the Mariner Wealth CEO who owns over 10% of Palmer Square Capital BDC, bought another 61,484 shares on July 6 at $10.47 to $10.56, about $647,000. A 10% owner adding in the open market at these levels is a straight bet on the credit book.
  • GF: Saba Capital took another $507,000 of New Germany Fund at $11.60 on July 2. Boaz Weinstein grinding a closed-end fund discount is a playbook, not a thesis, but he only grinds when the gap pays.
  • RIG: Transocean director Chad C. Deaton bought 35,000 shares at $4.95 on July 2, $173,250. A six-figure open-market buy from a board member in an out-of-favor offshore driller is exactly the kind of quiet print this tape exists to catch.

Filings window July 6 to July 8, 2026, sourced from SEC EDGAR. Not investment advice.

item.title: Charles Gillman Plants an Activist Flag in TransAct While YPF's Top Brass Buys Its Own Stock
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: A veteran small-cap activist files a fresh 13D on the casino-printer maker, YPF's chairman and upstream chief put half a million on the tape, and a Brazilian power director drops $4.3 million.
item.content:encoded:
A veteran small-cap activist files a fresh 13D on the casino-printer maker, YPF's chairman and upstream chief put half a million on the tape, and a Brazilian power director drops $4.3 million.

One real activist print, one C-suite cluster worth your attention, and a lot of plumbing to ignore. Here is the tape.

The 13D that matters

Charles Gillman filed a fresh SCHEDULE 13D on TransAct Technologies (TACT) on July 6 (filing). A 13D is the loud form, filed when you cross 5% of a company and intend to do something about it, and Gillman does not file quiet paperwork. He is a career small-cap activist who has fought his way onto dozens of microcap boards. TransAct makes transaction printers and terminals for casinos and restaurants, a sleepy niche with real customers, exactly the kind of underloved balance sheet activists like to shake.

The rest of the 13D pile was amendments from founders, family holders, and fund vehicles rebalancing their own paperwork. Nothing there changes a thesis.

Insiders: energy executives are writing checks

YPF (YPF) gave us the best cluster of the day (two or more insiders buying the same stock in the same window, which is rarer and louder than one). Chairman and CEO Horacio Daniel Marin bought 2,840 shares at $46.35 for $131,634 on July 2, and his VP of Upstream, Matias Osvaldo Farina, bought 8,875 shares across two prints for roughly $401,000 the same day. The two men running Argentina's flagship oil company put over half a million dollars of their own money into the stock in one session. I take that at face value.

Same sector, different hemisphere: Pedro Batista de Lima Filho, a director at AXIA Energia (AXIA3), Brazil's power giant, bought about 415,000 shares around $10.25 on July 1, roughly $4.3 million in total. That is the largest genuine open-market buy on the tape and it barely made a headline anywhere.

Angeliki Frangou did her usual thing at Navios Maritime Partners (NMM), buying 3,511 units across three prints between $71.59 and $74.48 for about $256,000. She buys her own units constantly, so one purchase proves little, but the pattern of a CEO steadily accumulating for years is its own signal.

A footnote on the other cluster: three officers of BlackRock Monticello Debt REIT bought $598,000 combined on July 1, every share at exactly $25.3754. Identical pricing means a subscription at NAV, not three people hitting the ask. File it away, do not trade it.

Ignore this one

SaverOne (SVRE) shows a $1.1 billion "insider buy" from VisionWave Holdings. That is merger mechanics, 326 million shares changing hands in a deal, not conviction. Skip it.

Filings from SEC EDGAR, July 5 to July 7, 2026. Not investment advice.

item.title: Wallbox's board and CEO just bought $5M together, and Uber quietly put $20M into Lime
item.dc:creator: Kairos
item.description: A board-plus-CEO cluster at multi-year lows is the only print that matters on a sleepy holiday tape. Two biotech 13Ds and a $6M director buy round it out.
item.content:encoded:
A board-plus-CEO cluster at multi-year lows is the only print that matters on a sleepy holiday tape. Two biotech 13Ds and a $6M director buy round it out.

Quiet holiday wire. No whale 13Ds, no early 13Fs, no Burry sightings. But one insider print today is the kind I actually care about, so let's get to it.

The only real story: WBX

WBX (Wallbox N.V.). Four insiders bought ~$5.0M of the EV-charging maker on the same day. Three directors at $1.36M each, plus CEO Enric Asuncion Escorsa adding $887k of his own. That is a cluster buy (multiple insiders buying together, the strongest insider signal there is), it is board-plus-CEO in unison, and it lands with the stock near multi-year lows. Insiders don't coordinate seven figures each into a falling chart for fun. Highest-conviction print on the tape, not close.

Fresh 13Ds

A 13D is an activist-scale stake, 5% or more with intent to do something about it. Two crossed today, both concentrated biotech money bottom-fishing:

  • CALC (CalciMedica). Bering Partners II GP filed a new SCHEDULE 13D on the immunology small-cap. First-time activist-scale money in a name this size gets my attention. Filing
  • PASG (Passage BIO). Lynx1 Capital Management opened a 13D on the gene-therapy name that has spent the year trading near cash value. Someone just decided the bottom of the range is investable. Filing

The rest of today's 13D wire was plumbing and I'm not making you read it: Gould Investors' twin filings on BRT Apartments and OLP (internal, affiliated), a Jefferies-to-Jefferies BDC shuffle, and a Hugreat/Alpex SPAC stake. Skip.

One amendment I do care about: LFCR (Lifecore Biomedical) drew same-day 13D/A activity from both 22NW and Legion Partners. No fresh escalation disclosed, but two activists touching the same small-cap on the same day means the seat-holders are still working.

The rest of the insider tape

  • CREX (Creative Realities). CEO Richard Mills bought $700k and CFO Tamra Koshewa $100k of the digital-signage micro-cap at $3.50. An $800k C-suite cluster in a company this small is real money to these people.
  • LIME (Neutron Holdings). Uber put $20M to work, 800k shares at $25, lifting its stake in the micromobility operator. A strategic corporate buyer averaging UP is a different animal than an insider buy, and the size demands notice.
  • BNR (Burning Rock Biotech). Director Yusheng Han disclosed $6.2M of open-market buying across the June 11 session in the China cancer-diagnostics name.
  • HDSN (Hudson Technologies). Hartree Partners, already a 10% owner, added $4.5M of the refrigerant reclaimer at ~$5.85.
  • BBD (Bank Bradesco). CLO Julio Cesar Bueno bought $2.0M of the Brazilian bank.
  • PSBD (Palmer Square Capital BDC). 10% owner Martin Bicknell added $845k near $10.55.
  • PSNY (Polestar). Three directors added a combined ~$78k. Small, but board buying is board buying.

Data window 2026-07-02 to 2026-07-04. Not investment advice.
